heroOverview: Macrophthalmos is a congenital condition in which one or both eyes are abnormally large at birth, with the axial length of the globe exceeding the age-adjusted upper limit of normal — roughly above 22–23 mm in a term neonate. The condition is rare and is distinct from buphthalmos, the globe enlargement caused by elevated intraocular pressure in congenital glaucoma, even though the two can look similar on clinical inspection. Simple macrophthalmos involves a large but otherwise well-formed eye; complex forms co-occur with high axial myopia, zonular weakness, lens subluxation, and an increased lifetime risk of retinal detachment. Many cases are associated with connective-tissue disorders such as Marfan syndrome, or with systemic overgrowth syndromes. Early ophthalmic assessment establishes the refractive error, screens for retinal complications, and guides genetic evaluation to identify treatable systemic disease.

keyStats.prevalence: Precise population prevalence is unknown; isolated macrophthalmos is rare, estimated at fewer than 1 per 50,000 births; syndromic forms depend on the underlying diagnosis
keyStats.demographics: Affects males and females; bilateral in the majority of Marfan-related and connective-tissue cases
keyStats.avgAge: Present at birth; may first be identified on newborn examination or when high myopia causes poor visual development in infancy
keyStats.globalCases: No reliable global incidence data; most epidemiological information derives from registry studies of associated syndromes such as Marfan syndrome (~1 per 5,000 births)

definition: Macrophthalmos (ICD-10: Q11.3) denotes a congenitally enlarged globe, with axial length exceeding the 97th percentile for gestational and postnatal age. In adults the upper limit of normal axial length is approximately 24–25 mm; in term neonates the mean is about 17 mm and isolated macrophthalmos is defined by length substantially above this range. The condition must be distinguished from buphthalmos, in which the enlargement is acquired due to high intraocular pressure in infancy stretching the immature sclera. Macrophthalmos proper arises from primary developmental overgrowth of the globe rather than from pressure. The majority of recognised cases occur in the context of connective-tissue disorders — chiefly Marfan syndrome (mutations in FBN1), Weill–Marchesani syndrome, and homocystinuria — which additionally cause zonular laxity permitting lens subluxation. Less commonly, macrophthalmos appears as an isolated finding or as part of chromosomal or somatic overgrowth syndromes. The enlarged globe with its thinner sclera and stretched zonular attachments creates several long-term risks: high axial myopia with retinal thinning, peripheral retinal degeneration, an elevated lifetime risk of rhegmatogenous retinal detachment, and — in Marfan syndrome — an approximately 50–80% prevalence of ectopia lentis. Ophthalmologists, medical geneticists, and cardiologists (for aortic surveillance in connective-tissue disease) participate in shared care.

typesClassification.description: The globe is enlarged without other systemic or ocular malformations; may be unilateral or bilateral; inheritance can be autosomal dominant in familial cases.
FBN1 mutations cause both globe enlargement and zonular fibril laxity; ectopia lentis is present in 50–80% and high axial myopia is universal; cardiovascular monitoring is mandatory.
Paradoxically, Weill–Marchesani syndrome can cause either microspherophakia or macrophthalmos depending on the variant; the enlarged globe has stiff connective tissue rather than laxity.
CBS gene mutations cause elevated plasma homocysteine, ectopia lentis, macrophthalmos, and a high risk of thromboembolic events; dietary and pharmacological treatment reduces complications.
When distinguishing from true primary macrophthalmos, corneal diameter, intraocular pressure, and Haab striae distinguish pressure-driven buphthalmos from congenitally large eyes.

primarySymptoms: Visibly enlarged globe — one or both eyes appear prominently large from birth or early infancy, often commented on by parents or detected during newborn examination.

High axial myopia — the elongated globe focuses images in front of the retina, causing blurred distance vision that may not be apparent until a child fails vision screening or shows disinterest in distant objects.
Poor visual development or nystagmus in bilateral high-myopia cases when the refractive error is uncorrected through infancy, depriving the visual cortex of clear input.
Lens subluxation (ectopia lentis) — in Marfan or homocystinuria cases, the crystalline lens tilts or shifts from its central position, causing fluctuating and distorted vision.
Strabismus — misalignment of the eyes arising from anisometropia (different refractive errors between the two eyes) or lens subluxation.
Photopsia (flashes of light) or floaters, which in a highly myopic elongated eye can signal vitreous traction or peripheral retinal degeneration and should prompt urgent retinal examination.
Corneal enlargement visible on inspection, which can accompany the enlarged globe, and may lead to confusion with congenital glaucoma.
Reduced visual acuity attributable to high myopia if spectacle correction is not prescribed early, driving amblyopia in the more affected eye.
Accommodative difficulty — children with lens subluxation experience fluctuating near-vision blur that varies with gaze direction and pupil size.
Deep anterior chamber and a large lens shadow on oblique illumination, signs suggestive of lens subluxation found on slit-lamp examination.
earlyWarningSigns: Noticeably prominent or large-appearing eyes observed by parents or by the midwife at birth.
An infant who does not track faces or fix steadily when the other eye is covered, suggesting high myopia or amblyopia.
Asymmetric red reflex on routine newborn check, with one reflex appearing different in quality — dimmer or shifted — suggesting lens subluxation.
A family history of Marfan syndrome, lens dislocation, or unexplained retinal detachment at a young age.
emergencySigns: Sudden onset of a shower of floaters, flashing lights, or a shadow across the visual field — classic symptoms of retinal detachment, a sight-threatening emergency requiring same-day retinal specialist review.
Acute reduction in vision combined with eye pain and redness in a patient with lens subluxation, raising concern for pupil block glaucoma needing urgent ophthalmological assessment.
Any sudden change in lens position or rapidly increasing subluxation, because dislocation of the lens into the anterior chamber or vitreous cavity can cause acute angle-closure glaucoma.

causes.description: Mutations in the fibrillin-1 gene weaken the microfibrillar scaffold of the sclera and zonules. Reduced scleral stiffness during fetal growth allows the globe to expand beyond normal limits while increased axial length drives high myopia and zonular laxity.

Cystathionine beta-synthase deficiency elevates plasma homocysteine, which cross-links zonular fibres and disrupts connective tissue. Globe enlargement, typically bilateral, accompanies ectopia lentis and a prothrombotic state.

Mutations in ADAMTS10 and related genes disrupt the extracellular matrix in ways that cause either short stature with microspherophakia or globe enlargement depending on the genetic variant and background.

In some familial cases, macrophthalmos segregates as an autosomal-dominant trait without a recognised syndrome, suggesting a gene variant affecting scleral growth control that has not yet been fully characterised.

Chromosomal duplications or segmental overgrowth syndromes occasionally include macrophthalmos as one of multiple affected organs; mechanism relates to broad dysregulation of growth factor signalling.

Focal scleral ectasia (staphyloma) concentrated at the posterior pole can give the impression of global enlargement on ophthalmoscopy; true global macrophthalmos is distinguished by measuring total axial length with ultrasonography.

riskFactors.description: Marfan syndrome is the most common identifiable cause; the condition follows autosomal-dominant inheritance with high penetrance, meaning a 50% per-pregnancy risk for affected parents.
An autosomal-recessive inborn error of metabolism; parental consanguinity doubles the risk; neonatal metabolic screening detects it early when programmes include homocystine.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of early-onset retinal detachment
riskFactors.description: A first-degree relative with juvenile retinal detachment or unexplained severe myopia suggests a heritable scleral connective-tissue disorder that may underlie macrophthalmos.

riskFactors.factor: High axial myopia in a parent
riskFactors.description: Severe myopia (below −8 D) has strong heritability and overlaps mechanistically with axial globe elongation; children of highly myopic parents need early ophthalmic screening.
Ehlers–Danlos syndrome, Stickler syndrome, and other heritable connective-tissue disorders are associated with scleral and zonular laxity that can present with macrophthalmos and high myopia.

Some series of isolated macrophthalmos show a modest male predominance, possibly linked to sex-linked variants in extracellular matrix genes, though the association is not consistent across all populations.

riskFactors.factor: Uncontrolled high intraocular pressure in infancy (risk of buphthalmos mimicking macrophthalmos)
riskFactors.description: Early-onset glaucoma left untreated stretches the infant sclera rapidly; prompt pressure reduction by surgery or medication limits globe enlargement — an important distinction because treatment differs entirely from primary macrophthalmos.

affectedDemographics: Infants and children with Marfan syndrome (prevalence approximately 1 per 5,000)
Individuals with homocystinuria detected on newborn metabolic screening
Children with connective-tissue syndromes including Stickler and Ehlers–Danlos
Males and females equally in most syndromic forms
diagnosisOverview: Macrophthalmos is suspected when clinical inspection reveals a conspicuously large globe, or when high axial myopia detected on cycloplegic refraction is disproportionate to age. B-scan ultrasonography confirms the diagnosis by measuring axial length; values above the age-adjusted 97th percentile establish a quantitative diagnosis. Slit-lamp examination assesses corneal diameter, anterior chamber depth, lens position, and zonular integrity — a key step in identifying ectopia lentis, which strongly suggests a connective-tissue aetiology. Dilated fundus examination with scleral indentation documents peripheral retinal thinning, lattice degeneration, and chorioretinal atrophy that predate retinal detachment. Intraocular pressure measurement and gonioscopy rule out congenital glaucoma (buphthalmos). Systemic evaluation depends on the clinical picture: in bilateral macrophthalmos with tall stature and aortic root dilation, the revised Ghent criteria guide Marfan diagnosis and mandate echocardiography. Plasma total homocysteine and urine amino acid chromatography screen for homocystinuria. Chromosomal microarray or exome sequencing is offered when syndromic features are present. Ophthalmoscopy with widefield imaging documents the retinal periphery, and optical coherence tomography (OCT) quantifies posterior retinal and choroidal thickness over the staphylomatous region. The main differentials are congenital glaucoma, high myopia without structural macrophthalmos, and Stickler syndrome.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures axial length to confirm globe enlargement and quantify the degree; also assesses vitreous for syneresis and looks for early posterior staphyloma.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Gel is placed on the closed eyelid and a small probe gently scanned over it; painless, takes about 10 minutes; contact immersion technique provides higher precision for axial length.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Accurately quantifies the refractive error without accommodation (which can mask the full degree of myopia); documents anisometropia between the two eyes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Eye drops dilate pupils and temporarily paralyse accommodation; a second refraction is performed 30–45 minutes after drops; near vision is blurred for several hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies ectopia lentis, documents corneal diameter (Haab striae in glaucoma), assesses anterior chamber depth, and detects iris transillumination defects from zonular weakness.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A specialised microscope illuminates the front of the eye while the patient rests a chin and forehead on supports; the clinician uses a bright narrow beam to examine each structure in turn.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents peripheral retinal thinning, lattice degeneration, chorioretinal atrophy, and posterior staphyloma — precursors to retinal detachment in highly myopic macrophthalmic eyes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: After pupil dilation, the ophthalmologist uses a headset-mounted or handheld lens; widefield cameras capture a 200-degree retinal view in a single photograph; takes 20–30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Plasma homocysteine and urine amino acid analysis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for homocystinuria, which presents with bilateral macrophthalmos, ectopia lentis, and a high thromboembolism risk; early dietary and pharmacological treatment reduces vascular events.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fasting blood sample and early-morning urine specimen; results typically available within a week from a metabolic laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: In suspected Marfan syndrome, documents aortic root diameter and valve morphology; aortic dissection is the most serious complication of unrecognised Marfan disease and is life-threatening.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A painless ultrasound of the chest taking about 45–60 minutes; gel is placed on the chest and a probe moved over the heart; no radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative genetic variant, confirms or excludes Marfan syndrome and homocystinuria at the molecular level, and guides cascade testing of family members.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample sent to a clinical genetics laboratory; results take 4–8 weeks.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of macrophthalmos focuses on three parallel priorities: correcting refractive error to prevent amblyopia, monitoring and preventing retinal detachment, and managing the systemic disorder when one is identified. Spectacles or contact lenses correct the high axial myopia as early as the first months of life; optical correction and patching therapy are used to treat associated amblyopia during the critical developmental window. Peripheral retinal lesions identified on wide-field fundoscopy — lattice degeneration, atrophic holes, traction tears — are treated prophylactically with laser photocoagulation or cryotherapy to seal the retina before it detaches. Retinal detachment, if it occurs, requires vitreoretinal surgery (scleral buckle, vitrectomy, or a combination) and carries a higher reoperation rate in highly myopic eyes than in emmetropic ones. Lens subluxation is initially managed with spectacle correction that accounts for the residual aphakic area; when subluxation progresses to cause uncontrollable glare, monocular diplopia, or pupil block, lens extraction using specialised low-volume vitrectomy technique with iris-sutured or scleral-fixated intraocular lens implantation is performed. For Marfan syndrome, beta-blocker or losartan therapy slows aortic root growth and reduces the risk of life-threatening aortic dissection. For homocystinuria, a pyridoxine trial, methionine-restricted diet, and betaine supplementation reduce plasma homocysteine and lower thrombosis risk. Genetic counselling provides recurrence risk information and enables cascade screening of at-risk family members.

medicalTreatments.name: High-myopia spectacle or contact lens correction
medicalTreatments.description: Myopia in macrophthalmic eyes can exceed −10 to −20 D; correction with spectacle lenses or rigid gas-permeable contact lenses from the first months of life is the most important single intervention to prevent amblyopia and support visual development.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Optical correction of high myopia reduces the amblyopia depth by an average of 2–4 logMAR lines in prospective series when started before age 2.

medicalTreatments.name: Patching therapy for amblyopia
medicalTreatments.description: When one eye carries more of the refractive burden, patching the better eye for several hours daily drives the visual cortex to process input from the affected eye during the sensitive period of neuroplasticity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves acuity by at least 2 logMAR lines in approximately 50–60% of children with refractive amblyopia when initiated before age 8.

medicalTreatments.name: Prophylactic laser photocoagulation or cryotherapy
medicalTreatments.description: Peripheral retinal breaks, lattice degeneration, and symptomatic vitreoretinal traction in the highly myopic macrophthalmic eye are treated with laser or cryotherapy to create a chorioretinal adhesion barrier preventing full retinal detachment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces progression to retinal detachment by approximately 50–60% at 5 years in treated lesions, based on retrospective controlled series.

medicalTreatments.name: Beta-blocker or losartan therapy (Marfan syndrome)
medicalTreatments.description: Atenolol or losartan reduce haemodynamic stress on the aortic root and slow fibrillin-mediated TGF-beta signalling; Marfan syndrome guidelines recommend one of these agents from the time of diagnosis to delay aortic surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Losartan slows aortic root growth rate by approximately 0.07 mm per year more than placebo in the COMPARE trial of Marfan patients; reduces surgical intervention risk by approximately 30–40% in long-term studies.

medicalTreatments.name: Pyridoxine, betaine, and methionine restriction (homocystinuria)
medicalTreatments.description: Pyridoxine (B6) supplementation restores residual CBS enzyme activity in responsive patients, reducing homocysteine levels; betaine and dietary methionine restriction address non-responsive cases; anticoagulation is added perioperatively.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pyridoxine-responsive patients achieve near-normal plasma homocysteine in approximately 50% of cases; treatment reduces the annual risk of thromboembolic events by roughly 50–80% compared with untreated patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Vitreoretinal surgery (scleral buckle, vitrectomy) for retinal detachment
surgicalOptions.description: Scleral buckling, pars plana vitrectomy, or a combined approach repairs rhegmatogenous retinal detachments in the highly myopic macrophthalmic eye; tamponade with gas or silicone oil supports the retina while the adhesion heals.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Single-surgery anatomical reattachment rates of approximately 70–85%; functional success (visual acuity improvement) is lower, around 50–65%, because of macular involvement and pre-existing myopic atrophy.

surgicalOptions.name: Lens extraction with scleral-fixated intraocular lens for ectopia lentis
surgicalOptions.description: When lens subluxation is severe, lens extraction with iris-sutured or scleral-fixated intraocular lens implantation removes the unstable lens and provides optical correction; performed via a small pars plana approach in experienced hands.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Final visual acuity of 20/40 or better is achieved in approximately 60–75% of cases; complication rate including retinal detachment is approximately 5–10% per procedure.

surgicalOptions.name: Prophylactic aortic root surgery (Marfan syndrome)
surgicalOptions.description: When aortic root diameter reaches 45–50 mm (lower threshold in women and those with rapid growth or family history of dissection), elective surgical replacement of the ascending aorta with a graft prevents catastrophic dissection.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Perioperative mortality in elective repair is approximately 1–2% at experienced centres; 10-year survival after elective repair exceeds 90%.

alternativeTreatments.name: Low-vision rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: For individuals with poor visual acuity despite optimal treatment, low-vision therapists prescribe magnifiers, tinted lenses, and electronic aids to maximise functional use of remaining vision.

alternativeTreatments.name: Genetic counselling and preimplantation genetic testing
alternativeTreatments.description: Families with identified Marfan or homocystinuria variants can undergo IVF with preimplantation genetic testing to select unaffected embryos, reducing the chance of transmission to the next generation.

whySeeSpecialist: Macrophthalmos requires evaluation by both a paediatric ophthalmologist and a clinical geneticist. The ophthalmologist quantifies the axial length, manages myopia-related complications, and monitors the retinal periphery for detachment precursors. The geneticist interprets molecular results, screens for life-threatening systemic associations — particularly cardiovascular disease in Marfan syndrome — and enables family cascade testing. Missing the Marfan diagnosis can be fatal if the aortic root is not monitored.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a paediatric or vitreoretinal ophthalmologist experienced in high myopia management and prophylactic retinal treatment for connective-tissue disorders. A medical geneticist or cardiologist with Marfan syndrome expertise is essential in syndromic cases. Centres affiliated with Marfan syndrome clinics can coordinate cardiology, ophthalmology, and genetics surveillance under one service.
preventionStrategies: Undergo genetic testing if a parent or sibling has Marfan syndrome, homocystinuria, or early retinal detachment so that offspring can be screened from birth.
Ensure newborn metabolic screening includes homocystinuria wherever programme coverage is available.
Attend annual dilated fundoscopy once high axial myopia is confirmed to identify peripheral retinal lesions before they cause detachment.
Avoid contact sports, high-impact activities, and Valsalva manoeuvres in Marfan syndrome unless cleared by a cardiologist, to reduce aortic stress.
Consider genetic counselling before pregnancy if a causative variant has been identified in the family.
lifestyleModifications: Wear the full spectacle or contact lens correction at all waking hours to maintain visual development and prevent amblyopia.
Report any new flashes of light, floaters, or a curtain over vision immediately to an ophthalmologist — these are retinal detachment symptoms needing same-day review.
Attend all scheduled retinal surveillance appointments, typically at least annually in highly myopic eyes.
Avoid contact sports, heavy weightlifting, and activities with a high risk of head injury when vitreoretinal traction is documented.
In Marfan syndrome, maintain beta-blocker or losartan therapy as prescribed and attend regular echocardiographic surveillance.

recommended: Methionine-restricted diet in homocystinuria to lower plasma homocysteine and reduce thromboembolism risk
Betaine-fortified foods or betaine supplements for non-pyridoxine-responsive homocystinuria
Adequate omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish to support retinal membrane integrity
Good hydration to reduce thrombosis risk in homocystinuria
avoid: High-methionine foods (large quantities of meat, eggs, and dairy) in homocystinuria
Foods and supplements that raise blood pressure in Marfan syndrome (very high caffeine or tyramine loads)
Vitamin B6 megadosing beyond prescribed therapeutic doses, which can cause peripheral neuropathy

exerciseGuidelines: In isolated macrophthalmos without systemic disease, physical activity is encouraged; protective polycarbonate spectacles are essential for contact sports. In Marfan syndrome, guidelines from the European Society of Cardiology recommend avoiding competitive sports and isometric activities until aortic dimensions and stability are confirmed. In homocystinuria, regular aerobic exercise supports vascular health but anaesthetic risk requires perioperative anticoagulation planning.
dailyManagement: Wear spectacle correction consistently and attend optical updates as myopia progresses.
Apply prescribed medical therapy — beta-blocker, losartan, or homocystinuria supplements — on schedule without interruption.
Keep a symptom diary noting any new floaters, flashes, or visual field changes to report at the next appointment.
Attend annual echocardiography and ophthalmology reviews as recommended by the multidisciplinary team.
Ensure schools and sports coaches are informed of the activity restrictions relevant to the underlying syndrome.
prognosis: The visual prognosis of macrophthalmos depends on whether retinal complications — particularly detachment and myopic macular atrophy — can be prevented or treated promptly. When high myopia is corrected optically from infancy and peripheral retinal lesions are treated prophylactically, a large proportion of children achieve good functional vision. Retinal detachment in highly myopic macrophthalmic eyes is more difficult to repair and carries a higher reoperation rate than in emmetropic eyes. The broader prognosis is shaped by the systemic condition: Marfan syndrome, with modern cardiovascular surveillance and elective aortic surgery when indicated, now has near-normal life expectancy in many cohorts; homocystinuria, if detected by newborn screening and treated from infancy, produces substantially better metabolic control and lower thromboembolic risk than late-diagnosed cases. Regular long-term ophthalmological follow-up is necessary throughout life because retinal detachment risk from axial myopia is lifelong.
recoveryTimeline: Amblyopia therapy in infancy is a years-long process completed by visual maturity around age 8–10. After prophylactic laser for retinal tears, the treated area becomes stable within 4–6 weeks and the patient can resume normal activities. After vitreoretinal surgery for detachment, face-down positioning may be required for 5–7 days, and return to sedentary activities typically takes 2–4 weeks. After lens extraction surgery, vision stabilises over 4–8 weeks as intraocular lens position settles.
complications: Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment — occurs in approximately 0.1–1% of highly myopic eyes per year; the lifetime cumulative risk is substantially higher in macrophthalmic eyes and requires urgent surgical repair.
Myopic macular atrophy and choroidal neovascularisation — progressive thinning of the posterior retina and choroid in heavily myopic eyes causes central visual loss that cannot be fully reversed.
Lens dislocation into the anterior chamber or vitreous — when zonular fibres fail completely, the subluxated lens dislocates, causing acute glaucoma or uveitis requiring urgent surgical removal.
Amblyopia — untreated high myopia or anisometropia during the critical period produces irreversible visual cortex underdevelopment that limits acuity in the affected eye.

Aortic dissection (Marfan syndrome) — without cardiovascular monitoring and timely surgery, the aortic root can dissect acutely, carrying a high mortality rate; it is the leading cause of early death in Marfan syndrome.

Thromboembolic events (homocystinuria) — elevated plasma homocysteine is a potent thrombogenic stimulus; stroke, pulmonary embolism, and limb thrombosis can occur at any age, especially perioperatively.

supportResources.name: The Marfan Foundation
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient and family resources on Marfan syndrome including heart, eye, and skeletal surveillance and support networks.

supportResources.name: National Eye Institute — Refractive Errors
supportResources.description: NIH guide to myopia, its management, and protective strategies for high-risk individuals.

supportResources.name: NHS — Marfan syndrome
supportResources.description: Plain-English UK overview of Marfan syndrome covering eye, heart, and skeletal involvement and multidisciplinary care.

confusedWithConditions.name: Congenital glaucoma (buphthalmos)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Congenital glaucoma enlarges the globe by raising intraocular pressure in infancy; it is distinguished from primary macrophthalmos by elevated IOP, corneal haziness, and Haab striae on slit-lamp examination.

confusedWithConditions.name: Microphthalmos
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Microphthalmos is the opposite malformation — a globe that is too small at birth; confusion arises when one eye appears large simply by comparison with a contralateral microphthalmic eye.

relatedConditions.name: Microphthalmos
relatedConditions.relevance: The complementary congenital globe-size disorder, sharing the same developmental spectrum and genetic assessment pathway.

relatedConditions.name: Marfan syndrome (ocular)
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common identifiable cause of syndromic macrophthalmos, requiring co-ordinated ophthalmological and cardiovascular surveillance.

relatedConditions.name: Marfan syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Underlying systemic condition in many macrophthalmos cases, with life-threatening aortic complications that require active management.

relatedConditions.name: Congenital glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Must be excluded as the cause of any enlarged infant globe because management is entirely different from primary macrophthalmos.

relatedConditions.name: Coloboma of the lens
relatedConditions.relevance: Lens coloboma and zonular defects co-occur with macrophthalmos in connective-tissue disorders.

faqs.question: What is macrophthalmos?
faqs.answer: Macrophthalmos is a congenital condition in which one or both eyes are abnormally large from birth. The axial length of the globe exceeds the upper limit of normal for age. It differs from congenital glaucoma, where raised pressure — not developmental overgrowth — enlarges the eye.

faqs.question: Is macrophthalmos the same as congenital glaucoma?
faqs.answer: No. Congenital glaucoma enlarges the eye by raising intraocular pressure in infancy, stretching the soft infant sclera. Primary macrophthalmos is a developmental overgrowth of the globe present without raised pressure. They look similar but require entirely different treatment.

faqs.question: What causes macrophthalmos?
faqs.answer: Common causes include Marfan syndrome (FBN1 gene mutation), homocystinuria (CBS gene mutation), and other connective-tissue disorders that weaken scleral stiffness. In some families, isolated macrophthalmos follows autosomal-dominant inheritance without a systemic syndrome.

faqs.question: Can a child with macrophthalmos see normally?
faqs.answer: With early optical correction for the associated high myopia and treatment of any amblyopia, many children achieve useful functional vision. The main threats to sight are retinal detachment and myopic macular atrophy, which require regular surveillance and prompt treatment.

faqs.question: What is Marfan syndrome and how does it cause macrophthalmos?
faqs.answer: Marfan syndrome is caused by mutations in the FBN1 gene, which weakens fibrillin microfibrils in connective tissue. In the eye, reduced scleral stiffness allows the globe to elongate during fetal growth, producing macrophthalmos and high axial myopia. Zonular laxity also causes lens subluxation in 50–80% of cases.

faqs.question: What is the risk of retinal detachment with macrophthalmos?
faqs.answer: Highly myopic macrophthalmic eyes have a substantially elevated lifetime risk of retinal detachment — estimated at 0.1–1% per year depending on axial length, peripheral retinal lesions, and vitreous status. Regular widefield fundoscopy and prophylactic laser treatment of retinal tears reduce this risk.

faqs.question: How is macrophthalmos diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is confirmed by B-scan ultrasonography measuring axial globe length above the age-adjusted normal range. Slit-lamp examination assesses lens position and corneal diameter. Systemic evaluation, including cardiac imaging and genetic testing, identifies underlying syndromes.

faqs.question: Is macrophthalmos inherited?
faqs.answer: It can be. Marfan syndrome follows autosomal-dominant inheritance (50% risk per pregnancy). Homocystinuria is autosomal recessive (25% risk when both parents carry the variant). Isolated familial macrophthalmos also follows dominant inheritance in some pedigrees. Genetic counselling clarifies the specific risk.

faqs.question: What treatment is available for macrophthalmos?
faqs.answer: Treatment focuses on correcting high myopia with spectacles or contacts, treating amblyopia with patching, monitoring the retinal periphery, and treating peripheral breaks prophylactically with laser. For Marfan syndrome, beta-blocker or losartan therapy protects the aorta, and lens surgery is used when subluxation is severe.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of retinal detachment to watch for?
faqs.answer: New sudden floaters, flashes of light, or a shadow or curtain moving across the visual field are warning signs of retinal detachment. Any of these symptoms requires same-day ophthalmological assessment, because earlier surgery gives better visual outcomes.

faqs.question: Does macrophthalmos affect the heart?
faqs.answer: Not directly, but in Marfan syndrome — the most common systemic cause of macrophthalmos — the aortic root is at risk of enlargement and dissection. All patients with macrophthalmos and features of Marfan syndrome need echocardiography and regular cardiology follow-up.

faqs.question: Can macrophthalmos be detected before birth?
faqs.answer: Large orbital diameters can be detected on prenatal ultrasound from about 20 weeks, particularly when bilateral. Fetal MRI may add detail. Genetic testing can identify Marfan or homocystinuria variants in high-risk families before birth.

faqs.question: What is ectopia lentis and how is it treated?
faqs.answer: Ectopia lentis is a displacement of the crystalline lens from its central position due to weakened zonular fibres. It causes fluctuating, distorted vision. Initially managed with spectacles, it requires surgical lens extraction with an intraocular implant when it causes uncontrolled glare, monocular diplopia, or pupil block.

faqs.question: Is high myopia in macrophthalmos treatable?
faqs.answer: The myopia is managed rather than cured. Spectacles or rigid contact lenses provide the refractive correction needed for clear vision. Refractive laser surgery is generally not used in high macrophthalmic myopia because the sclera is already thin and at risk. Low-vision aids help when acuity is reduced.

faqs.question: Can macrophthalmos worsen over time?
faqs.answer: The globe size at birth is fixed, but axial length continues to increase with normal eye growth through childhood. In highly myopic macrophthalmic eyes, progressive elongation further increases retinal and choroidal stretch, and myopic atrophy can progress into adulthood even after growth ceases.

faqs.question: What sports or activities should be avoided?
faqs.answer: Contact sports, heavy weightlifting, and activities with a risk of head trauma increase the risk of retinal detachment in highly myopic eyes and should be avoided or performed with protective eyewear. In Marfan syndrome, competitive sports and extreme isometric exercise require cardiological clearance.

faqs.question: What is homocystinuria and why does it matter in macrophthalmos?
faqs.answer: Homocystinuria is a metabolic disorder caused by CBS gene mutations that raise plasma homocysteine, damaging connective tissue and blood vessel walls. In the eye it causes macrophthalmos and lens subluxation; systemically it causes a high risk of stroke and blood clots. Newborn screening and early dietary treatment substantially reduce complications.

faqs.question: Will my child with macrophthalmos need glasses?
faqs.answer: Almost certainly, because the elongated globe causes high myopia. Spectacles or contact lenses should be prescribed as early as the diagnosis is confirmed — often in the first months of life — to prevent amblyopia and support normal visual development during the critical period.

faqs.question: What does a genetics referral involve?
faqs.answer: A clinical genetics appointment includes detailed family history, physical examination, chromosomal microarray, and targeted gene sequencing for FBN1, CBS, and related genes. Results guide systemic screening, treatment decisions, and inform other family members of their carrier status or risk.

metaDescription: Macrophthalmos is a congenital large-eye malformation linked to Marfan syndrome and high myopia. Learn causes, diagnosis and treatment options.
